title: Recover Risk-Neutral Density (Breeden-Litzenberger)

source.file: content/A Practical Guide To Quantitative Finance Interviews Book by Xinfeng Zhou (chapters)/chapter-06-finance.md

setup.narrative: European call price: c(K) = exp(-rT) * E[(S_T - K)^+] = exp(-rT) * integral over s from K to infinity of (s - K) * f(s) ds, where f is the risk-neutral density of S_T. Suppose c(K) is observable for ALL strikes K.
rules: Differentiate c(K) once with respect to K: dc/dK = -exp(-rT) * (1 - F(K)) where F is the CDF.
Differentiate again: d^2 c / dK^2 = exp(-rT) * f(K).
Use Leibniz's rule for differentiating an integral with K-dependent bounds and integrand.
setup.question: Given the full strike profile c(K), how do we recover f(K)?

learningObjectives: Differentiate an integral with K-dependent limits and integrand (Leibniz).
Recognize the second derivative of c with respect to K isolates f(K).
Appreciate the practical implication: option prices encode the implied risk-neutral distribution.

stages.label: First derivative
stages.prompt: c(K) = exp(-rT) * integral_K^infinity of (s - K) * f(s) ds. Compute dc/dK using Leibniz's rule.

options.text: dc/dK = exp(-rT) * [-(K - K) * f(K) - integral_K^infinity f(s) ds] = -exp(-rT) * (1 - F(K)) where F is the CDF.

stages.explanation: Leibniz: differentiate an integral with bounds and integrand depending on K. Lower bound term: -(s-K)|_{s=K} * f(K) = 0. Integrand term: integral of d/dK[(s-K) f(s)] ds = -integral_K^infinity f(s) ds = -(1 - F(K)). Multiply by exp(-rT): dc/dK = -exp(-rT) * (1 - F(K)).

stages.label: Second derivative
stages.prompt: dc/dK = -exp(-rT) * (1 - F(K)). Differentiate once more in K.

options.text: d^2 c / dK^2 = -exp(-rT) * (-f(K)) = exp(-rT) * f(K).  So f(K) = exp(rT) * d^2 c / dK^2.

stages.explanation: d/dK[1 - F(K)] = -f(K). So d^2 c / dK^2 = -exp(-rT) * (-f(K)) = exp(-rT) * f(K). Rearranging: f(K) = exp(rT) * d^2 c / dK^2. This is the Breeden-Litzenberger formula.

stages.label: Commit
stages.template: f_ST(K) = exp(__[a]__T) * d^2 c / dK^2.  This recovers the __[b]__-neutral density from European call prices observed at all strikes.
stages.explanation: f_ST(K) = exp(rT) * d^2c/dK^2. The risk-neutral density at level K is encoded in the convexity of the call-strike curve. This is foundational for model calibration and inferring market views on tail risk.

stages.label: Reflection
prompts: Why does this work for the RISK-NEUTRAL density rather than the real-world one? (Because c is computed under Q, the risk-neutral measure.)
How would you implement this in practice when only finitely many strikes are quoted? (Smooth interpolation or a parametric vol surface, then differentiate twice.)

What does Breeden-Litzenberger tell you about a butterfly spread (long c(K-h) - 2*c(K) + c(K+h))? (Approximately h^2 * c''(K) ≈ h^2 * exp(-rT) * f(K), so a tight butterfly is like buying a small strip of probability mass at K.)
